title: Cat Urinating Pink or Red: Causes and Urgent Next Steps
meta_desc: Discover what it means when your cat has pink or red urine. Learn why this is a high-priority veterinary situation and when to seek emergency care.
content_html:
What Does Pink or Red Urine Mean?

Finding pink or red-tinted urine in your cat's litter box is naturally alarming. This coloration typically indicates the presence of blood, a condition known as hematuria. It can be caused by various issues, including urinary tract infections (UTIs), feline idiopathic cystitis, or more seriously, the presence of urinary crystals and bladder stones.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

We categorize pink or red urine as a high-priority situation. While it can sometimes be a manageable infection, it is often the first sign of a dangerous urinary blockage, especially in male cats. If your cat is straining, vocalizing in the litter box, or producing little to no urine, seek emergency veterinary care immediately. A blockage is life-threatening and can lead to kidney failure within hours.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Your Vet

If your cat has an accident on a light-colored surface or you notice the color on a puppy pad, take a clear photo. This visual evidence helps our veterinary team determine the concentration of blood and provides a baseline for monitoring. If possible, note how many times they are visiting the box to help us triage the urgency level more accurately during your intake.

faqs.question: Can stress cause blood in my cat's urine?
faqs.answer: Yes, Feline Idiopathic Cystitis (FIC) is a common condition often triggered by stress that causes inflammation and bleeding in the bladder. It still requires a veterinary exam to ensure there is no blockage.

faqs.question: Is it okay to wait until morning if my cat is acting normal?
faqs.answer: If your cat is eating, drinking, and urinating a normal volume, you may be able to wait for a next-day appointment. However, if they are straining or seem in pain, you must visit an emergency clinic immediately.

faqs.question: Does red urine always mean a UTI?
faqs.answer: No. While infections are possible, cats are more prone to sterile cystitis or crystals. A urinalysis at the clinic is the only way to determine the exact cause.
